| 3D MLC NOR | 100,000-1,000,000 |  |

However, by applying certain algorithms and design paradigms such as [wear leveling](/wiki/Wear_leveling "Wear leveling") and [memory over-provisioning](/wiki/Flash_over-provisioning "Flash over-provisioning"), the endurance of a storage system can be tuned to serve specific requirements.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

In order to compute the longevity of the NAND flash, one must account for the size of the memory chip, the type of memory (e.g. SLC/MLC/TLC), and use pattern. Industrial NAND and server NAND are in demand due to their capacity, longer endurance and reliability in sensitive environments.

As the number of bits per cell increases, performance and life of NAND flash may degrade, increasing random read times to 100μs for TLC NAND which is 4 times the time required in SLC NAND, and twice the time required in MLC NAND, for random reads.[[71]](#cite_note-Vättö-71)

## Flash file systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Flash file systems")]

Main article: [Flash file system](/wiki/Flash_file_system "Flash file system")

Because of the particular characteristics of flash memory, it is best used with either a controller to perform wear leveling and error correction or specifically designed flash file systems, which spread writes over the media and deal with the long erase times of NOR flash blocks. The basic concept behind flash file systems is the following: when the flash store is to be updated, the file system will write a new copy of the changed data to a fresh block, remap the file pointers, then erase the old block later when it has time.

In practice, flash file systems are used only for [memory technology devices](/wiki/Memory_technology_device "Memory technology device") (MTDs), which are embedded flash memories that do not have a controller. Removable flash [memory cards](/wiki/Memory_card "Memory card"), SSDs, [eMMC](/wiki/EMMC "EMMC")/[eUFS](/wiki/Universal_Flash_Storage "Universal Flash Storage") chips and [USB flash drives](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive") have built-in [controllers](/wiki/Flash_memory_controller "Flash memory controller") to perform wear leveling and error correction so use of a specific flash file system may not add benefit.

## Capacity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Capacity")]

Multiple chips are often arrayed or die stacked to achieve higher capacities[[173]](#cite_note-173) for use in consumer electronic devices such as multimedia players or [GPSs](/wiki/GPS "GPS"). The capacity scaling (increase) of flash chips used to follow [Moore's law](/wiki/Moore%27s_law "Moore's law") because they are manufactured with many of the same [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuits "Integrated circuits") techniques and equipment. Since the introduction of 3D NAND, scaling is no longer necessarily associated with Moore's law since ever smaller transistors (cells) are no longer used.

Consumer flash storage devices typically are advertised with usable sizes expressed as a small integer power of two (2, 4, 8, etc.) and a conventional designation of megabytes (MB) or gigabytes (GB); e.g., 512 MB, 8 GB. This includes [SSDs](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") marketed as hard drive replacements, in accordance with traditional [hard drives](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive"), which use [decimal prefixes](/wiki/SI_prefix "SI prefix").[[174]](#cite_note-174) Thus, an SSD marked as "64 [GB](/wiki/Gigabyte "Gigabyte")" is at least 64 × 10003 bytes (64 GB). Most users will have slightly less capacity than this available for their files, due to the space taken by file system metadata and because some operating systems report SSD capacity using [binary prefixes](/wiki/Binary_prefix "Binary prefix") which are somewhat larger than conventional prefixes .

The flash memory chips inside them are sized in strict binary multiples, but the actual total capacity of the chips is not usable at the drive interface. It is considerably larger than the advertised capacity in order to allow for distribution of writes ([wear leveling](/wiki/Wear_leveling "Wear leveling")), for sparing, for [error correction codes](/wiki/Error_correction_codes "Error correction codes"), and for other [metadata](/wiki/Metadata "Metadata") needed by the device's internal firmware.

In 2005, Toshiba and [SanDisk](/wiki/SanDisk "SanDisk") developed a NAND flash chip capable of storing 1 GB of data using [multi-level cell](/wiki/Multi-level_cell "Multi-level cell") (MLC) technology, capable of storing two bits of data per cell. In September 2005, [Samsung Electronics](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") announced that it had developed the world's first 2 GB chip.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

In March 2006, Samsung announced flash hard drives with capacity of 4 GB, essentially the same order of magnitude as smaller laptop hard drives, and in September 2006, Samsung announced an 8 GB chip produced using a 40 nm manufacturing process.[[176]](#cite_note-176)
In January 2008, SanDisk announced availability of their 16 GB MicroSDHC and 32 GB SDHC Plus cards.[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-178)

More recent flash drives (as of 2012) have much greater capacities, holding 64, 128, and 256 GB.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

A joint development at Intel and Micron will allow the production of 32-layer 3.5 terabyte (TB[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]) NAND flash sticks and 10 TB standard-sized SSDs. The device includes 5 packages of 16 × 48 GB TLC dies, using a floating gate cell design.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

Flash chips continue to be manufactured with capacities under or around 1 MB (e.g. for BIOS-ROMs and embedded applications).

In July 2016, Samsung announced the 4 TB[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Samsung 850 EVO which utilizes their 256 Gbit 48-layer TLC 3D V-NAND.[[181]](#cite_note-181) In August 2016, Samsung announced a 32 TB 2.5-inch SAS SSD based on their 512 Gbit 64-layer TLC 3D V-NAND. Further, Samsung expects to unveil SSDs with up to 100 TB of storage by 2020.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

## Transfer rates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Transfer rates")]

Flash memory devices are typically much faster at reading than writing.[[183]](#cite_note-Master-183) Performance also depends on the quality of storage controllers, which become more critical when devices are partially full.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*][[183]](#cite_note-Master-183) Even when the only change to manufacturing is die-shrink, the absence of an appropriate controller can result in degraded speeds.[[184]](#cite_note-184)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Applications")]

### Serial flash

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Serial flash")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/IPhone_3G_teardown_-_Silicon_Storage_Tech_SST25VF080B-3309.jpg/250px-IPhone_3G_teardown_-_Silicon_Storage_Tech_SST25VF080B-3309.jpg)](/wiki/File:IPhone_3G_teardown_-_Silicon_Storage_Tech_SST25VF080B-3309.jpg)

Serial Flash: Silicon Storage Tech SST25VF080B

Serial flash is a small, low-power flash memory that provides only serial access to the data - rather than addressing individual bytes, the user reads or writes large contiguous groups of bytes in the address space serially. [Serial Peripheral Interface Bus](/wiki/Serial_Peripheral_Interface_Bus "Serial Peripheral Interface Bus") (SPI) is a typical protocol for accessing the device.
When incorporated into an [embedded system](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system"), serial flash requires fewer wires on the [PCB](/wiki/Printed_circuit_board "Printed circuit board") than parallel flash memories, since it transmits and receives data one bit at a time. This may permit a reduction in board space, power consumption, and total system cost.

There are several reasons why a serial device, with fewer external pins than a parallel device, can significantly reduce overall cost:

* Many [ASICs](/wiki/Application-specific_integrated_circuit "Application-specific integrated circuit") are pad-limited, meaning that the size of the [die](/wiki/Die_(integrated_circuit) "Die (integrated circuit)") is constrained by the number of [wire bond](/wiki/Wire_bond "Wire bond") pads, rather than the complexity and number of gates used for the device logic. Eliminating bond pads thus permits a more compact integrated circuit, on a smaller die; this increases the number of dies that may be fabricated on a [wafer](/wiki/Wafer_(electronics) "Wafer (electronics)"), and thus reduces the cost per die.
* Reducing the number of external pins also reduces assembly and [packaging](/wiki/IC_package "IC package") costs. A serial device may be packaged in a smaller and simpler package than a parallel device.
* Smaller and lower pin-count packages occupy less PCB area.
* Lower pin-count devices simplify PCB [routing](/wiki/Routing_(EDA) "Routing (EDA)").

There are two major SPI flash types. The first type is characterized by small blocks and one internal SRAM block buffer allowing a complete block to be read to the buffer, partially modified, and then written back (for example, the Atmel [AT45](/wiki/AT45 "AT45") *DataFlash* or the [Micron Technology](/wiki/Micron_Technology "Micron Technology") Page Erase NOR Flash). The second type has larger sectors where the smallest sectors typically found in this type of SPI flash are 4 KB, but they can be as large as 64 KB. Since this type of SPI flash lacks an internal SRAM buffer, the complete block must be read out and modified before being written back, making it slow to manage. However, the second type is cheaper than the first and is therefore a good choice when the application is code shadowing.

The two types are not easily exchangeable, since they do not have the same pinout, and the command sets are incompatible.

Most [FPGAs](/wiki/FPGA "FPGA") are based on SRAM configuration cells and require an external configuration device, often a serial flash chip, to reload the configuration [bitstream](/wiki/Bitstream "Bitstream") every power cycle.[[185]](#cite_note-Clive_Maxfield-185)

#### Firmware storage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Firmware storage")]

With the increasing speed of modern CPUs, parallel flash devices are often much slower than the memory bus of the computer they are connected to. Conversely, modern [SRAM](/wiki/Static_RAM "Static RAM") offers access times below 10 [ns](/wiki/Nanosecond "Nanosecond"), while [DDR2 SDRAM](/wiki/DDR2_SDRAM "DDR2 SDRAM") offers access times below 20 ns. Because of this, it is often desirable to [shadow](/wiki/Shadow_RAM "Shadow RAM") code stored in flash into RAM; that is, the code is copied from flash into RAM before execution, so that the CPU may access it at full speed. Device [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware") may be stored in a serial flash chip, and then copied into SDRAM or SRAM when the device is powered-up.[[186]](#cite_note-186) Using an external serial flash device rather than on-chip flash removes the need for significant process compromise (a manufacturing process that is good for high-speed logic is generally not good for flash and vice versa). Once it is decided to read the firmware in as one big block it is common to add compression to allow a smaller flash chip to be used. Since 2005, many devices use serial NOR flash to deprecate parallel NOR flash for firmware storage. Typical applications for serial NOR flash include storing firmware for [hard drives](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive"), [BIOS](/wiki/BIOS "BIOS"), [Option ROM](/wiki/Option_ROM "Option ROM") of [expansion cards](/wiki/Expansion_card "Expansion card"), [DSL modems](/wiki/DSL_modem "DSL modem"), etc.

### Flash memory as a replacement for hard drives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Flash memory as a replacement for hard drives")]

Main article: [Solid-state drive](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Intel_525_mSATA_SSD.jpg/250px-Intel_525_mSATA_SSD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Intel_525_mSATA_SSD.jpg)

An Intel mSATA SSD in 2020

One more recent application for flash memory is as a replacement for [hard disks](/wiki/Hard_disk "Hard disk"). Flash memory does not have the mechanical limitations and latencies of hard drives, so a [solid-state drive](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") (SSD) is attractive in terms of speed, noise, power consumption, and reliability. Flash drives are gaining traction as mobile device secondary storage devices; they are also used as substitutes for hard drives in high-performance desktop computers and some servers with [RAID](/wiki/RAID "RAID") and [SAN](/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network") architectures.

There remain some aspects of flash-based SSDs that make them unattractive. The cost per gigabyte of flash memory remains significantly higher than that of hard disks.[[187]](#cite_note-187) Also, flash memory has a finite number of P/E (*program/erase*) cycles, but this seems to be currently under control since warranties on flash-based SSDs are approaching those of current hard drives.[[188]](#cite_note-188) In addition, deleted files on SSDs can remain for an indefinite period of time before being overwritten by fresh data; erasure or shred techniques or software that work well on magnetic hard disk drives have no effect on SSDs, compromising security and forensic examination. However, due to the so-called *[TRIM](/wiki/Trim_(computing)#SD/MMC "Trim (computing)")* command employed by most solid state drives, which marks the logical block addresses occupied by the deleted file as unused to enable [garbage collection](/wiki/Garbage_collection_(computing) "Garbage collection (computing)"), data recovery software is not able to restore files deleted from such.

For relational databases or other systems that require [ACID](/wiki/ACID "ACID") transactions, even a modest amount of flash storage can offer vast speedups over arrays of disk drives.[[189]](#cite_note-189)

In May 2006, [Samsung Electronics](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") announced two flash-memory based PCs, the Q1-SSD and Q30-SSD were expected to become available in June 2006, both of which used 32 GB SSDs, and were at least initially available only in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[190]](#cite_note-190) The Q1-SSD and Q30-SSD launch was delayed and finally was shipped in late August 2006.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

The first flash-memory based PC to become available was the Sony Vaio UX90, announced for pre-order on 27 June 2006 and began to be shipped in Japan on 3 July 2006 with a 16 GB flash memory hard drive.[[192]](#cite_note-192) In late September 2006 Sony upgraded the flash-memory in the Vaio UX90 to 32 GB.[[193]](#cite_note-193)

A solid-state drive was offered as an option with the first [MacBook Air](/wiki/MacBook_Air "MacBook Air") introduced in 2008, and from 2010 onwards, all models were shipped with an SSD. Starting in late 2011, as part of [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel")'s [Ultrabook](/wiki/Ultrabook "Ultrabook") initiative, an increasing number of ultra-thin laptops are being shipped with SSDs standard.

There are also hybrid techniques such as [hybrid drive](/wiki/Hybrid_drive "Hybrid drive") and [ReadyBoost](/wiki/ReadyBoost "ReadyBoost") that attempt to combine the advantages of both technologies, using flash as a high-speed non-volatile [cache](/wiki/Cache_(computing) "Cache (computing)") for files on the disk that are often referenced, but rarely modified, such as application and operating system [executable](/wiki/Executable "Executable") files.

On [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), the NAND flash products are used as file storage device, for example, [eMMC](/wiki/EMMC "EMMC") and [eUFS](/wiki/EUFS "EUFS").

### Flash memory as RAM

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Flash memory as RAM")]

As of 2012,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit) there are attempts to use flash memory as the main computer memory, [DRAM](/wiki/Dynamic_random-access_memory "Dynamic random-access memory").[[194]](#cite_note-194)

### Archival or long-term storage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Archival or long-term storage")]

Floating-gate transistors in the flash storage device hold charge which represents data. This charge gradually leaks over time, leading to an accumulation of [logical errors](/wiki/Data_integrity#Logical_integrity "Data integrity"), also known as "[bit rot](/wiki/Data_rot "Data rot")" or "bit fading".[[195]](#cite_note-ni12-195)

#### Data retention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Data retention")]

It is unclear how long data on flash memory will persist under archival conditions (i.e., benign temperature and humidity with infrequent access with or without prophylactic rewrite). Datasheets of Atmel's flash-based "[ATmega](/wiki/ATmega "ATmega")" microcontrollers typically promise retention times of 20 years at 85 °C (185 °F) and 100 years at 25 °C (77 °F).[[196]](#cite_note-196)

The retention span varies among types and models of flash storage. When supplied with power and idle, the charge of the transistors holding the data is routinely refreshed by the [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware") of the flash storage.[[195]](#cite_note-ni12-195) The ability to retain data varies among flash storage devices due to differences in firmware, [data redundancy](/wiki/Data_redundancy "Data redundancy"), and [error correction](/wiki/Error_correction "Error correction") algorithms.[[197]](#cite_note-197)

An article from [CMU](/wiki/Carnegie_Mellon_University "Carnegie Mellon University") in 2015 states "Today's flash devices, which do not require flash refresh, have a typical retention age of 1 year at room temperature." And that retention time decreases exponentially with increasing temperature. The phenomenon can be modeled by the [Arrhenius equation](/wiki/Arrhenius_equation "Arrhenius equation").[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)

While flash storage retains data for a longer time if stored at colder temperatures, a higher but not extreme temperature while writing reduces stress and wear on the drive, given that electrons are able to flow more easily, according to Tim Schulte, Pranav Kalavade, and Johnmichael Hands from Intel.[[200]](#cite_note-200)

### FPGA configuration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: FPGA configuration")]

Some [FPGAs](/wiki/FPGA "FPGA") are based on flash configuration cells that are used directly as (programmable) switches to connect internal elements together, using the same kind of floating-gate transistor as the flash data storage cells in data storage devices.[[185]](#cite_note-Clive_Maxfield-185)

In addition to individual flash memory chips, flash memory is also [embedded](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system") in [microcontroller](/wiki/Microcontroller "Microcontroller") (MCU) chips and [system-on-chip](/wiki/System-on-chip "System-on-chip") (SoC) devices.[[223]](#cite_note-Yiu-227) Flash memory is embedded in [ARM chips](/wiki/ARM_chips "ARM chips"),[[223]](#cite_note-Yiu-227) which have sold 150 billion units worldwide as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit),[[224]](#cite_note-228) and in [programmable system-on-chip](/wiki/Programmable_system-on-chip "Programmable system-on-chip") (PSoC) devices, which have sold 1.1 billion units as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit).[[225]](#cite_note-229) This adds up to at least 151.1 billion MCU and SoC chips with embedded flash memory, in addition to the 45.4 billion known individual flash chip sales as of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit), totalling at least 196.5 billion chips containing flash memory.

## Flash scalability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flash_memory&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Flash scalability")]

See also: [List of semiconductor scale examples](/wiki/List_of_semiconductor_scale_examples "List of semiconductor scale examples") and [Moore's law](/wiki/Moore%27s_law "Moore's law")

Due to its relatively simple structure and high demand for higher capacity, NAND flash memory is the most aggressively [scaled technology](/wiki/List_of_semiconductor_scale_examples "List of semiconductor scale examples") among [electronic devices](/wiki/Electronic_devices "Electronic devices"). The heavy competition among the top few manufacturers only adds to the aggressiveness in shrinking the [floating-gate MOSFET](/wiki/Floating-gate_MOSFET "Floating-gate MOSFET") design rule or process technology node.[[107]](#cite_note-Kawamatus-107) While the expected shrink timeline is a factor of two every three years per the original version of [Moore's law](/wiki/Moore%27s_law "Moore's law"), this has recently been accelerated in the case of NAND flash to a factor of two every two years.

As the [MOSFET](/wiki/MOSFET "MOSFET") feature size of flash memory cells reaches the 15–16 nm minimum limit, further flash density increases will be driven by TLC (3 bits/cell) combined with vertical stacking of NAND memory planes. The decrease in endurance and increase in uncorrectable bit error rates that accompany feature size shrinking can be compensated by improved error correction mechanisms.[[231]](#cite_note-235) Even with these advances, it may be impossible to economically scale flash to smaller and smaller dimensions as the number of electron holding capacity reduces. Many promising new technologies (such as [FeRAM](/wiki/Ferroelectric_RAM "Ferroelectric RAM"), [MRAM](/wiki/Magnetoresistive_Random_Access_Memory "Magnetoresistive Random Access Memory"), [PMC](/wiki/Programmable_metallization_cell "Programmable metallization cell"), [PCM](/wiki/Phase-change_memory "Phase-change memory"), [ReRAM](/wiki/Resistive_random-access_memory "Resistive random-access memory"), and others) are under investigation and development as possible more scalable replacements for flash.[[232]](#cite_note-236)
